name: code-refactoring-tech-debt
description: You are a technical debt expert specializing in identifying, quantifying, and prioritizing technical debt in software projects. Analyze the codebase to uncover debt, assess its impact, and create acti
capabilities: Scan codebases for code duplication, complexity issues, and structural problems
Calculate financial impact of technical debt with cost projections
Generate prioritized remediation roadmaps based on ROI analysis
Create measurable metrics dashboards for tracking debt reduction
Provide incremental refactoring strategies with implementation examples
Design prevention strategies including automated quality gates and debt budgets
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analysis detected 30 potential issues (external commands, weak crypto, system reconnaissance) but all are false positives from markdown code examples and documentation text. No executable code, network requests, or security threats found. The skill contains only documentation about technical debt analysis with code block examples.
valueStatement: Identify and quantify technical debt that slows development. Get actionable remediation plans with ROI projections to prioritize improvements that maximize team velocity.
limitations: Cannot execute code or run automated scans - relies on codebase exploration and analysis
ROI projections are estimates based on typical development costs
Does not replace dedicated static analysis tools like SonarQube or dependency auditors
Requires human judgment to balance technical debt against business priorities

useCases.title: Legacy Codebase Assessment
useCases.description: Development teams inherit legacy projects with accumulated technical debt and need a comprehensive analysis to understand what issues exist, their impact on velocity, and a prioritized plan for improvements.

useCases.title: Technical Debt Prioritization
useCases.description: Engineering managers must decide where to invest limited resources for maximum impact. This skill quantifies the cost of debt items and ranks them by ROI to justify technical work to stakeholders.

useCases.title: Refactoring Planning
useCases.description: Developers planning major refactoring efforts need structured approaches to break down large changes into incremental steps, measure progress, and prevent introducing new debt during the process.

promptTemplates.title: Quick Debt Scan
promptTemplates.prompt: Scan this codebase for technical debt issues. Focus on code duplication, high complexity functions, and missing tests. Provide a summary of the top 10 issues with their locations.
promptTemplates.scenario: Rapid assessment to identify major problems

promptTemplates.title: Comprehensive Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Perform a complete technical debt inventory covering code debt, architecture debt, testing gaps, and documentation issues. Calculate the annual cost of each category and create a prioritized remediation plan with quick wins.
promptTemplates.scenario: Full audit for planning major initiatives

promptTemplates.title: Specific Component Deep Dive
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze the [component/service] for technical debt. Identify complexity hotspots, coupling issues, and test coverage gaps. Propose a refactoring strategy with incremental implementation steps.

promptTemplates.title: Remediation Roadmap
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a quarterly remediation roadmap based on the technical debt found. Include quick wins (1-2 weeks), medium-term improvements (1-3 months), and long-term initiatives (3-6 months) with ROI projections for each.

bestPractices: Always include test coverage when refactoring - write tests first (TDD) to prevent regressions
Use feature flags for gradual rollout of refactored code to enable quick rollbacks
Document architectural decisions (ADRs) to explain why changes were made for future maintainers
antiPatterns: Big bang refactoring - avoid rewriting entire systems at once. Use incremental changes with continuous delivery
Refactoring without measurements - establish baseline metrics before starting to quantify improvement
Ignoring business value - not all technical debt needs fixing. Prioritize by impact on velocity and risk

faq.question: What types of technical debt can this skill detect?
faq.answer: The skill identifies code debt (duplication, complexity, poor structure), architecture debt (design flaws, outdated dependencies), testing debt (coverage gaps, brittle tests), documentation debt, and infrastructure debt (deployment issues, missing monitoring).

faq.question: How accurate are the ROI projections?
faq.answer: ROI projections are estimates based on typical development costs (e.g., $150/hour) and industry averages for bug fix times. Use them as relative comparisons between debt items rather than absolute predictions. Adjust the cost assumptions to match your team's actual rates.

faq.question: Can this skill automatically fix the technical debt it finds?
faq.answer: No, this skill analyzes and plans but does not execute changes. It provides detailed refactoring strategies, code examples, and implementation guidance that you or your team can apply. For automated fixes, look into dedicated refactoring tools.

faq.question: How do I prioritize which technical debt to address first?
faq.answer: Focus on quick wins (high value, low effort) that provide immediate velocity improvements. Then tackle items with the highest calculated annual cost. Consider risk level too - critical security issues should be prioritized even if ROI is lower. The skill provides a ranked roadmap based on these factors.

faq.question: What's the difference between this and static analysis tools like SonarQube?
faq.answer: Static analysis tools provide automated scans with rule-based detection. This skill offers semantic analysis, contextual understanding, and strategic planning. It can interpret findings, calculate business impact, and create human-readable remediation plans. Use both together: tools for scanning, this skill for analysis and planning.

faq.question: How often should I perform technical debt analysis?
faq.answer: Run a full analysis quarterly to track progress and identify new debt. Perform targeted scans before major refactoring efforts or when velocity noticeably decreases. Establish continuous monitoring with automated quality gates (pre-commit hooks, CI checks) to catch debt early as described in the prevention strategy.
